coredata.dc:title: Bifunctional ratiometric sensor based on highly fluorescent nitrogen and sulfur biomass-derived carbon nanodots fabricated from manufactured dairy product as a precursor
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Mohamed, Rasha M.K.
Mohamed, Sabrein H.
Asran, Aml M.
Alsohaimi, Ibrahim H.
Hassan, Hassan M.A.
Ibrahim, Hossieny
El-Wekil, Mohamed M.
coredata.dc:description: Novel biomass-derived carbon dots co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur were fabricated through facile and simple synthetic method from manufactured milk powder and methionine as precursors. The as-fabricated platform was used for ratiometric fluorescence sensing of Cu (II) and bisphosphonate drug risedronate sodium. The sensing platform is based on oxidation of o-phenylenediamine by Cu (II) to form 2, 3-diaminophenazine (oxidized product) with an emission peak at 557 nm. The resultant product quenched the fluorescence emission of as-fabricated carbon dots at 470 nm through Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner-filter effect (IFE). Upon addition of risedronate sodium, the formation of 2, 3-diaminophenazine was decreased as a result of Cu (II) chelation with risedronate sodium, recovering the fluorescence emission of carbon dots. The ratio of fluorescence at 470 nm and 557 nm was measured as a function of Cu (II) and risedronate sodium concentrations. The proposed sensing platform sensitively detected Cu (II) and risedronate sodium in the range of 0.01–55 μM and 5.02–883 µM with LODs (S/N = 3) of 0.003 μM and 1.48 µM, respectively. The sensing platform exhibited a good selectivity towards Cu (II) and risedronate sodium. The sensing system was used to determine Cu (II) and risedronate sodium in different sample matrices with recoveries % in the range of 99–103 % and 97.4–103.8 %, and RSDs % in the range of 1.5–3.0 % and 1.8–3.6 %, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cu (II)
Bisphosphonate
Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped biomass-derived carbon dots
o-Phenylendiamine
Ratiometric fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of nitrogen and sulfur biomass-derived carbon dots
Real sample preparation and quantum yield of nitrogen and sulfur biomass-derived carbon dots
TEM, DLS, FTIR, EDX, and XRD of nitrogen and sulfur biomass-derived carbon dots
XPS characterization of nitrogen and sulfur biomass-derived carbon dots
Optical properties of nitrogen and sulfur biomass-derived carbon dots
Stability of the nitrogen and sulfur biomass-derived carbon dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, (b) DLS, and (c) FT-IR of the as-fabricated nitrogen and sulfur biomass-derived carbon dots while (d) Raman spectra of un-doped carbon dots and nitrogen/sulfur biomass-derived carbon dots, and (e) EDX of nitrogen/sulfur biomass-derived carbon dots.

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of nitrogen and sulfur biomass-derived carbon dots (insets are pictures under UV-light and day-light). (b) Independency of emission on the excitation wavelengths.

(A) Effect of different Cu (II) concentrations on the emission intensity of nitrogen and sulfur biomass-derived carbon dots + o-phenylenediamine system while insets are images of sensing system under different concentrations of Cu (II) under UV light. (B) Calibration plot between F557/F470 and concentration of Cu (II) under optimum conditions.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of risedronate sodium different concentrations on the emission intensity of nitrogen and sulfur biomass-derived carbon dots + 55.0 µM Cu (II) + o-phenylenediamine system while insets are images of sensing system under different concentrations of risedronate sodium under UV light. (B) Calibration plot between F470/F557 and concentration of risedronate sodium under optimum conditions.

Influence of metal ions on detecting Cu (II) in (A) absence and (B) presence of Cu (II). Concentrations of these potential interfering species were as follows: metal ions were 350 μM and Cu (II) = 10 µM while (C) and (D) are the influence of 5.0 mM ions and biomolecules on the detection of risedronate sodium in absence and presence of risedronate sodium, respectively. Concentration of risedronate sodium is 441.3 µM.
Steps for the preparation of nitrogen and sulfur biomass-derived carbon dots and their utilization for the ratiometric fluorescence detection of Cu (II) and risedronate sodium.
Analytical parameters for comparison between sensing system and other methods for Cu (II) and risedronate sodium.

Precision of the proposed fluorometric sensing system for the determination of Cu (II) (n = 4).
Precision of the proposed fluorometric sensing system for the determination of risedronate sodium (n = 4).

head.ce:title: Bifunctional ratiometric sensor based on highly fluorescent nitrogen and sulfur biomass-derived carbon nanodots fabricated from manufactured dairy product as a precursor

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel biomass-derived carbon dots co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur were fabricated through facile and simple synthetic method from manufactured milk powder and methionine as precursors. The as-fabricated platform was used for ratiometric fluorescence sensing of Cu (II) and bisphosphonate drug risedronate sodium. The sensing platform is based on oxidation of o-phenylenediamine by Cu (II) to form 2, 3-diaminophenazine (oxidized product) with an emission peak at 557 nm. The resultant product quenched the fluorescence emission of as-fabricated carbon dots at 470 nm through Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner-filter effect (IFE). Upon addition of risedronate sodium, the formation of 2, 3-diaminophenazine was decreased as a result of Cu (II) chelation with risedronate sodium, recovering the fluorescence emission of carbon dots. The ratio of fluorescence at 470 nm and 557 nm was measured as a function of Cu (II) and risedronate sodium concentrations. The proposed sensing platform sensitively detected Cu (II) and risedronate sodium in the range of 0.01–55 μM and 5.02–883 µM with LODs (S/N = 3) of 0.003 μM and 1.48 µM, respectively. The sensing platform exhibited a good selectivity towards Cu (II) and risedronate sodium. The sensing system was used to determine Cu (II) and risedronate sodium in different sample matrices with recoveries % in the range of 99–103 % and 97.4–103.8 %, and RSDs % in the range of 1.5–3.0 % and 1.8–3.6 %, respectively.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped biomass-derived carbon dots

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper ion is an essential component, being involved in various important activities and plant photosynthesis. Deficiency of Cu (II) ions in the human body fluids leads to many serious diseases such as fragile bones, blood anemia, gastrointestinal problems, and myeloneuropathy [1–3]. Deficiency of Cu (II) ions in plants leads to the decrease of their yields [4]. The concentration of about 0.04 mg/L Cu (II) is an important for normal metabolism [5]. Excessive intake of Cu (II) may cause serious conditions such as genetic disorders, Wilson’s disease, neurodegenerative disorders, and protein denaturation [6–8]. Based on the recommendation by U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (U.S. EPA), the Cu (II) ions concentration in the potable water should not exceed 20 µM [9]. The most common traditional methods used for detection of Cu (II) are atomic-absorption [10,11] and ICP-MS [12,13], which have some disadvantages such as high cost instrumentation, need highly skilled persons, and exhaustive pretreatment steps. Therefore, it is essential to build-up a selective, sensitive, and cost-effective sensors for the estimation of Cu (II).

Risedronate sodium is used for the treatment of bone-metastasis, osteoporosis, Paget disease of the bone, and malignancy-mediated hypercalcemia [14,15]. Analytical survey revealed that risedronate sodium has been determined using HPLC-UV [16,17], CE [18], and LC-MS [19]. These techniques are complicated, require exhaustive pretreatment steps, and need highly skilled operators. Moreover, the spectrophotometric methods used for the quantitation of risedronate sodium suffer from many drawbacks such as corrosive reagents, pre-derivatization reactions, and slow-reactions [20,21]. As a result, it is necessary to develop sensitive, fast-response, and reliable sensors for the determination of risedronate sodium in biological fluids and during quality-control applications. It is noteworthy mentioning that this paper is the first report for the fluorometric determination of risedronate sodium.

Recently, carbon dots (C-dots) have gained more attention in many fields such as drug-delivery, sensors, catalysis, and bio-imaging [22–24]. These applications are attributed to their unique properties including ease of surface modification, high water dispersion, strong biocompatibility, and optical/chemical stability [25–27]. Doping of C-dots with heteroatoms such as boron (B), nitrogen (N), and sulfur (S) can enhance the electronic/optical and surface modification of C-dots [28–30]. The nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S) atoms are widely used as dopants due to similarity to carbon (C) atom in the form of atomic radius and electronegativity, respectively [31,32].

Ratiometric emission probes have drawn great attentions in the field of analysis as they offer more accurate measurements than conventional emission probes. They employ the ratio of two-isolated fluorescent peaks of different wavelengths. They have several advantages over conventional emission probes such as the reduction of background interferences and elimination of the fluctuation in the detection conditions [33,34].

Herein, a dual-function nitrogen and sulfur biomass-derived carbon dots was proposed for sensitive and selective detection of Cu (II) and risedronate sodium. The sensing platform is based on the oxidation of o-phenylendiamine to 2, 3-diaminophenazine by Cu (II). The carbon dots and 2, 3-diaminophenazine emitted blue and yellow fluorescent lights at 470 nm and 557 nm, respectively, after excitation at 383 nm. Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner-filter effect (IFE) occurred between carbon dots and 2, 3-diaminophenazine in the sensing system, resulted in diminishing the fluorescence emission of carbon dots. In the presence of risedronate sodium, the formation of 2, 3-diaminophenazine was decreased due to the chelation between Cu (II) and risedronate sodium. This strong binding resulted in a limited fluorescence quenching, and hence an increase in the fluorescence ratio (F470/F557).
Details of materials, reagents, and instrumentation were listed in Electronic Supplementary materials (ESM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of nitrogen and sulfur biomass-derived carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Hydrothermal method was used to fabricate nitrogen and sulfur biomass-derived carbon dots after optimization of synthesis procedures as follows: 2.3 g milk powder and 0.6 g methionine were dissolved in 30 mL DDW, followed by heating at 220 °C for 3 h. After that, the product was subjected to centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 25 min, filtered, and finally dialyzed (M.wt = 3000). Then, the final solution was freeze-dried (Scheme 1 ).

For detection of Cu(II) ion: 15 µL of 1.5 mM o-phenylenediamine and different amounts of Cu (II) were thoroughly mixed, and then completed to 1.3 mL with acetate buffer (pH 4.0). Then, the above solution was incubated at room temperature (RT) for 20 min. After that, 200 µL of 3.0 mg mL−1 of the as-synthesized carbon dots was added and left at RT for 5 min. Finally, the fluorescence ratio was measured at 470 nm and 557 nm after excitation at 383 nm (Scheme 1).

For detection of risedronate sodium: 15 µL of 1.5 mM o-phenylenediamine, 55 µM Cu (II), and different amounts of risedronate sodium were completed to 1.3 mL with acetate buffer (pH 4.0). Then, the above solution was incubated at RT for 20 min. After that, 200 µL of 3.0 mg mL−1 of the as-synthesized carbon dots was added and left at RT for 5 min. Finally, the fluorescence ratio was measured at 470 nm and 557 nm after excitation at 383 nm (Scheme 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real sample preparation and quantum yield of nitrogen and sulfur biomass-derived carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Steps for preparation of real samples were detailed in ESM according to reports [35–38]. In addition, the quantum yield calculation of as-fabricated carbon dots was detailed in ESM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TEM, DLS, FTIR, EDX, and XRD of nitrogen and sulfur biomass-derived carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 a, the TEM image exhibits the spherical shape of the as-fabricated carbon dots with a good dispersion and average diameter of 3.4 nm, confirmed by dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Fig. 1b). The functional groups on the surface of carbon dots were examined using FT-IR where the band at 3330 cm−1 is related to υ (OH, NH), while weak bands at 2730–2850 cm−1 are related to υ (–CH2) [39,40]. Moreover, the intense band at 1710 cm−1 is assigned to the υ (CO, CN), while weak bands at 1455 cm−1, 1375 cm−1, and 997 cm−1 are attributed to υ (CS), υ (CN), and υ (CO), respectively [41,42], (Fig. 1c). Raman spectra exhibited two main bands at 1395 and 1595 cm−1, which are assigned to the D- and G-bands, respectively (Fig. 1d). It was found that the intensity ratio of D/G bands for un-doped C-dots is 0.87, while for N/S co-doped biomass carbon dots is 0.95, indicating that nitrogen and sulfur biomass-derived carbon dots have more disordered carbon structures than un-doped C-dots [43]. Fig. 1e exhibits the EDX of carbon dots with sharp peaks of C, O, N, and S. Fig. S1 shows the XRD of as-fabricated carbon dots with diffraction peak at 23.4°, which is assigned to the (002) plane of graphite, suggesting a graphene-like structure [44,45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: XPS characterization of nitrogen and sulfur biomass-derived carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Full characterization of XPS spectra was introduced in ESM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of nitrogen and sulfur biomass-derived carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Optical properties of the as-fabricated carbon dots were investigated using UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. 2 a shows that the N/S co-doped biomass carbon dots have two absorption bands at 230 nm and 315 nm, which assign to CC and CO functional groups, respectively. Moreover, the fluorescence spectra show that the emission wavelength of nitrogen and sulfur biomass-derived carbon dots is located at 470 nm after excitation at 383 nm. Fig. 2b exhibits different emission spectra of carbon dots at different excitation wavelengths between 360 and 410 nm, suggesting the independence of emission on the excitation wavelengths. The quantum emission yield of the prepared nitrogen and sulfur biomass-derived carbon dots was calculated to be 32.7 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of the nitrogen and sulfur biomass-derived carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Factors affecting stability of nitrogen and sulfur biomass-derived carbon dots were described in ESM with corresponding Figs. (S3a-S3c).

To obtain the superb analytical performance of the proposed method, the experimental conditions affecting the determination of Cu (II) and risedronate sodium were optimized including pH value, incubation time, and excitation wavelength. The influence of the pH value on the detection of Cu (II) was examined in the pH range of 2.0–9.0. In the presence of Cu (II), it was found that the fluorescence ratio was decreased at pH value more than 7, which may be attributed to Cu (II) precipitation in pH greater than 7 [37,38]. In the following experiments, pH 4.0 was chosen for the detection of Cu (II) (Fig. S4a). Moreover, the incubation time was examined where it was found that 5 min were sufficient to reach maximum and stable fluorescence ratio (Fig. S4b). Further, the optimum conditions for the detection of risedronate sodium were obtained where it was found that the highest fluorescence ratio in the presence of risedronate sodium was obtained at pH = 4.0 (Fig. S5a). It was reported that bisphosphonate containing drugs e.g. risedronate sodium form stable complex with Cu (II) in acidic pH [46]. Moreover, when the pH is alkaline, the formation of 2, 3-diaminophenazine was hindered. Thus, pH 4.0 was selected for the determination of risedronate sodium in further steps. The effect of incubation time was also studied where it was found that highest and stable fluorescence ratio was obtained after 5 min (Fig. S5b). Therefore, 5 min was taken as an ideal incubation time for the detection of Cu (II) and risedronate sodium. The excitation of the system was adjusted at λexc. = 383 nm to obtain comparable fluorescence emissions of nitrogen and sulfur biomass-derived carbon dots and 2, 3-diaminophenazine at 470 nm and 557 nm, respectively (Fig. S6).

Fluorescence spectroscopy, UV/Vis absorption spectroscopy, and fluorescence decay were used to clarify the sensing mechanism of the proposed probe. It was seen from the fluorescence spectra that the emission intensities of nitrogen and sulfur biomass-derived carbon dots at 470 nm were slightly changed by the addition of Cu (II) and o-phenylenediamine. Mixing of Cu (II) and o-phenylenediamine resulted in diminishing the fluorescence emission of nitrogen and sulfur biomass-derived carbon dots at 470 nm while an emission peak was appeared at 557 nm, which assigned to the oxidation of o-phenylenediamine by Cu (II). It was found that the risedronate sodium has no fluorescence emission, but it can chelate Cu (II), recovering the emission of the as-prepared carbon dots (Fig.S7a). In addition, the oxidation of o-phenylenediamine by Cu (II) resulted in the formation of a new absorption peak at 425 nm, which was decreased after addition of risedronate sodium (Fig. S7b). Fig. S7c exhibits the overlapping between the absorption spectrum of 2, 3-diaminophenazine overlaps well with the emission spectrum of carbon dots, suggesting the possible inner-filter effect (IFE) between carbon dots and 2, 3-diaminophenazine. Fig. S7d shows that the fluorescence lifetimes of nitrogen and sulfur biomass-derived carbon dots were changed after incubation with Cu (II)/ o-phenylenediamine, suggesting a dynamic quenching [46]. Moreover, the chemical interaction (π-π stacking) between the carbon dots and 2, 3-diaminophenazine may lead to Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET). It can be observed in Fig. S8 that the absorption peaks corresponding to the aromatic υ (CH) at 3030 cm−1, υ (CC of aromatic ring) at 1510 cm−1, and δ(CC of aromatic ring) at 760 cm−1 of the oxidized o-phenylenediamine [47] emerged in the FT-IR spectrum of the testing system containing nitrogen and sulfur biomass-derived carbon dots, Cu (II), and o-phenylenediamine. As a conclusion, the quenching of the fluorescence by 2, 3-diaminophenazine may be attributed to FRET and IFE. After adding risedronate sodium to this testing system, however, these absorptions were depressed due to the inhibition of generating the oxidized o-phenylenediamine by risedronate sodium.
The proposed method was validated according to ICH-guidelines [48] as follows:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Linearity, limit of detection (LOD), and limit of quantitation (LOQ)
ce:section.ce:para: Moreover, the sensing platform was used to detect risedronate sodium starting with Cu (II) concentration of 55 µM, which gave the highest emission intensity of 2, 3-diaminophenazine (at 557 nm) and lowest emission intensity of nitrogen and sulfur biomass-derived carbon dots (at 470 nm). Based on the chelation between risedronate sodium and Cu (II), the emission intensity of fluorescent probe was recovered (increased) while the emission intensity of 2, 3-diaminophenazine was decreased in the range 26.5–883 µM (Fig. 4 A). A linear plot was obtained between F470/F557 and concentration of risedronate sodium with a regression equation of F470/F557 = 0.023 + 0.011C risedronate sodium (R2 = 0.9990) with LOD and LOQ of 1.48 µM and 5.02 µM, respectively (Fig. 4B). Table 1 shows a comparison between analytical parameters of this sensing system and other methods.

Accuracy of the proposed method was assessed by the analysis of Cu (II) and risedronate sodium in water, pharmaceutical, serum, and urine samples. Prior to testing, biological fluids (human serum and urine) were diluted 50 times to decrease interference. Tables 2 and 3
show that recoveries % for all samples are limited in acceptable ranges, confirming that the proposed sensing platform has promising applications for the determination of Cu (II) and risedronate sodium. Moreover, the proposed method was compared with reported methods for the determination of Cu (II) and risedronate sodium, confirming the accuracy of the proposed fluorometric ratiometric sensor.

Intraday precision of the proposed method for sensing of both Cu (II) and risedronate sodium was evaluated at four different concentrations on the same day, while inter-day precision was assessed on three consecutive days (Tables 4 and 5
). The acceptable recoveries % and RSDs % values suggested the reliability of the proposed method for sensing applications.

Selectivity of the sensing platform was evaluated towards Cu (II) and risedronate sodium in the presence of common interfering ions and bio-substances. The results showed that no serious interference from common ions and bio-substances (Fig. 5 ). It is noteworthy to mention that the sensing system is susceptible to oxidation by Ag+ and Fe3+. Thus, KCl and NaF were added to mask interferences from Ag+ and Fe3+, respectively. Moreover, biological samples were treated with maleimide to prevent interference from bithiols such as Glut and Cys, which are able to complex Cu (II).

The optimum conditions used for the fluorometric sensing of Cu (II) and risedronate sodium have been slightly varied to test the robustness of the fluorometric sensing method. Small and intended variations in the pH value, concentration of o-phenylendiamine, incubation time, and incubation temperature. The acceptable values of recoveries % and RSDs % value confirmed the robustness of the proposed sensing method (Table S1 and S2).

Herein, a ratiometric fluorescence probe was fabricated for sensing of Cu (II) and risedronate sodium. The as-fabricated nitrogen and sulfur biomass-derived carbon dots showed excellent optical and fluorescence properties. The analytical sensing system is based on the oxidation of o-phenylenediamine by Cu (II) to generate 2, 3-diaminophenazine, which induced fluorescence emission at 557 nm. The oxidized product quenched the fluorescence of nitrogen and sulfur biomass-derived carbon dots at 470 nm via FRET and IFE. Upon addition of risedronate sodium, a strong Cu (II) chelator, the formation of 2, 3-diaminophenazine was decreased. Excellent selectivity, good sensitivity, and reliability are the main advantages of the proposed sensing system. Importantly, the sensing system was successfully applied for the determination of Cu (II) in environmental water samples; and risedronate sodium in pharmaceutical tablets and biological fluids with acceptable results.

Rasha M.K. Mohamed: Investigation, Software, Validation, Visualization. Sabrein H. Mohamed: Investigation, Software, Validation, Visualization. Aml M. Asran: Investigation, Software, Validation, Visualization. Ibrahim H. Alsohaimi: Investigation, Software, Validation, Visualization. Hassan M.A. Hassan: Investigation, Software, Validation, Visualization. Hossieny Ibrahim: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Mohamed M. El-Wekil: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the Deanship of Scientific Research at Jouf University under Grant Number (DSR2022-RG-0135).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.122444.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
